title: Conceptual Overview of Included Norms
text: document outlines set lexical semantic norms included dataset clarifies measure represents. brings together constructs different areas psycholinguistics—covering familiarity, processing, meaning, embodiment, affect, lexical form—describes defined typically interpreted. goal provide clear reference using variables, noting related measures overlap capture distinct aspects word knowledge.

title: Conceptual Overview of Included Norms
text: Age acquisition (aoa) estimates word first understood. often treated developmental property lexical knowledge: early-acquired words tend entrenched, fluent, easier access. Age acquisition overlaps frequency prevalence, reducible either. word can common adult text learned late, relatively infrequent known early childhood experience. Word prevalence (prevalence_pknown, prevalence_score) estimates widely word known across speakers. differs token frequency. Frequency asks often word occurs corpus; prevalence asks many people know . especially useful stimulus selection rare words equally obscure, common-looking words may vary population-level familiarity. English Lexicon Project measures (lexdec_rt, lexdec_naming_rt) behavioral indices lexical access. Lexical decision time reflects quickly readers judge letter string word, naming latency reflects quickly can read word aloud. measures downstream outcomes many lexical properties, including frequency, age acquisition, orthographic/phonological form, semantic richness, affective sensorimotor associations.

title: Conceptual Overview of Included Norms
text: Concreteness (concreteness) measures much word’s meaning refers something can directly experienced senses action. Concrete words object names tend perceptual grounding; abstract words depend language, concepts, relations, definitions. Imageability (imageability) measures easily word gives rise mental image. closely related concreteness, two . abstract emotional words can imageable, concrete referents may relatively difficult picture. Imageability evoked mental imagery, simply whether referent exists physical world. Sensory experience rating (ser) broadens idea imagery beyond visual imageability. asks strongly word evokes sensory perceptual experience across modalities. conceptually close imageability sensorimotor strength, general “can picture ?” can include sound, taste, touch, smell, sensory impressions.

title: Conceptual Overview of Included Norms
text: Lancaster sensorimotor norms describe perceptual action systems associated concept. perceptual dimensions (lancaster_visual, lancaster_auditory, lancaster_haptic, lancaster_gustatory, lancaster_olfactory, lancaster_interoceptive) estimate strongly concept experienced different sensory modalities. action dimensions (lancaster_hand_arm, lancaster_foot_leg, lancaster_mouth, lancaster_head, lancaster_torso) estimate bodily effector involvement. Body-object interaction (boi) related specific construct: easily body can physically interact referent word. often high manipulable objects lower large, distant, diffuse, abstract referents. BOI overlaps concreteness hand/arm sensorimotor strength, targets affordance bodily interaction rather general perceptual vividness. verbs--space dimensions (vis_upwrd, vis_dwnwrd, vis_vert, vis_left, vis_right, vis_horiz, vis_toward, vis_away) capture spatial schemas associated actions. dimensions useful verbs whose meanings imply direction, orientation, motion. extend embodied semantics object properties sensory modalities action structure spatial representation.

title: Conceptual Overview of Included Norms
text: Valence, arousal, dominance (valence, arousal, dominance) capture affective meaning. Valence measures pleasantness, arousal measures emotional activation intensity, dominance measures sense control power evoked word. dimensions related separable: word can unpleasant high-arousal, pleasant low-arousal, emotionally intense without implying personal control. Humor (humor) captures perceived funniness humorous association. adjacent affect, especially valence arousal, simply positivity. humorous words may negative, absurd, taboo, playful, surprising. makes humor useful distinct evaluative pragmatic dimension. Socialness (socialness) measures much word’s meaning concerns people, social interaction, roles, institutions, norms, groups, social ideas. concreteness valence: social concepts can abstract, concrete, positive, negative, emotionally neutral, emotionally loaded. Socialness helps separate interpersonal cultural meaning general semantic richness affect. Gender connotation (gender_femininity) measures perceived masculine--feminine association. culturally mediated semantic dimension rather grammatical property. can useful studying social meaning, stereotype content, political language, distributional biases lexical materials.

what: Lexical Form, Polysemy, and Supplementary Structure
title: Conceptual Overview of Included Norms
text: Wordset-derived measures (n_defs, n_pos) describe dictionary structure word-form level. Definition count rough indicator semantic richness polysemy, part--speech count captures grammatical flexibility. wide data also includes ws_pos, semicolon-separated list dictionary POS labels, wordset_index keeps POS-specific definition counts. reference metadata, human ratings. norming studies present words without sentence context explicit sense disambiguation, POS-specific dictionary entries interpreted identifying sense grammatical use rated. GloVe embeddings (glove50) provide distributional semantic vectors words compiled vocabulary. norming ratings, offer complementary corpus-based representation lexical meaning. can used estimate semantic similarity, find neighbors, compare human-rated semantic dimensions distributional structure.

title: Conceptual Overview of Included Norms
text: Several included norms cluster around broad conceptual families: groupings interpretive rather fixed. example, humor affective also social pragmatic; BOI sensorimotor also related concreteness; prevalence lexical availability often predicts lexical access; imageability sits semantic grounding sensory experience. value unified resource overlaps can modeled directly rather treated interchangeable labels.

title: lexis Demo
text: Use lex_lookup() retrieve values one words. Use wide = TRUE one row per word.
code: lex_lookup(c("dog", "justice"), dims = c("aoa", "concreteness", "valence")) #> # A tibble: 6 × 9 #>   word    lemma   dataset    dimension  mean    sd n_ratings scale_min scale_max #>   <chr>   <chr>   <chr>      <chr>     <dbl> <dbl>     <int>     <dbl>     <dbl> #> 1 dog     dog     aoa        aoa        2.8   1.2         20         1        25 #> 2 justice justice aoa        aoa        9.47  2.76        19         1        25 #> 3 dog     dog     concreten… concrete…  4.85  0.46        27         1         5 #> 4 justice justice concreten… concrete…  1.45  0.74        29         1         5 #> 5 dog     dog     warriner_… valence    7     2.07        42         1         9 #> 6 justice justice warriner_… valence    6.8   1.54        41         1         9 lex_lookup(c("dog", "justice"), wide = TRUE) #> # A tibble: 2 × 30 #>   word    lemma     aoa   boi concreteness gender_femininity humor #>   <chr>   <chr>   <dbl> <dbl>        <dbl>             <dbl> <dbl> #> 1 dog     dog      2.8    6.4         4.85              5.22 NA    #> 2 justice justice  9.47  NA           1.45              4.87  1.93 #> # ℹ 23 more variables: lancaster_auditory <dbl>, lancaster_gustatory <dbl>, #> #   lancaster_haptic <dbl>, lancaster_interoceptive <dbl>, #> #   lancaster_olfactory <dbl>, lancaster_visual <dbl>, #> #   lancaster_foot_leg <dbl>, lancaster_hand_arm <dbl>, lancaster_head <dbl>, #> #   lancaster_mouth <dbl>, lancaster_torso <dbl>, lexdec_rt <dbl>, #> #   lexdec_naming_rt <dbl>, prevalence_pknown <dbl>, prevalence_score <dbl>, #> #   prevalence_freq_zipf <dbl>, ser <dbl>, socialness <dbl>, valence <dbl>, …

title: lexis Demo
text: lex_search() helps find words pattern. lex_filter() filters numeric dimensions.
code: lex_search("^bio", cols = "word") #> # A tibble: 164 × 2 #>    word         lemma        #>    <chr>        <chr>        #>  1 biological   biological   #>  2 biology      biology      #>  3 bio          bio          #>  4 biopsy       biopsy       #>  5 biography    biography    #>  6 biologist    biologist    #>  7 biochemical  biochemical  #>  8 bionic       bionic       #>  9 biochemistry biochemistry #> 10 biographer   biographer   #> # ℹ 154 more rows lex_filter(concreteness > 4, aoa < 8) #> # A tibble: 2,515 × 75 #>    word   lemma    aoa   boi concreteness gender_femininity humor imageability #>    <chr>  <chr>  <dbl> <dbl>        <dbl>             <dbl> <dbl>        <dbl> #>  1 can    can     4.32  4.88         4.55             NA    NA             416 #>  2 man    man     3.11  5.35         4.79              6.4   2.10          620 #>  3 people people  3.52  6.08         4.82              4.19  2.19          568 #>  4 night  night   3.61  2.13         4.52             NA     1.92           NA #>  5 talk   talk    3.68  4.07         4.07             NA    NA             467 #>  6 guy    guy     4.11  5.84         4.68              5.85  2.47           NA #>  7 money  money   5.11  6.29         4.54              4.63  2.25           NA #>  8 girl   girl    4     5.52         4.85             NA     2.65           NA #>  9 father father  4.11  6.04         4.52             NA     2.44           NA #> 10 boy    boy     3.67  4.96         4.76             NA     2.59           NA #> # ℹ 2,505 more rows #> # ℹ 67 more variables: lancaster_auditory <dbl>, lancaster_gustatory <dbl>, #> #   lancaster_haptic <dbl>, lancaster_interoceptive <dbl>, #> #   lancaster_olfactory <dbl>, lancaster_visual <dbl>, #> #   lancaster_foot_leg <dbl>, lancaster_hand_arm <dbl>, lancaster_head <dbl>, #> #   lancaster_mouth <dbl>, lancaster_torso <dbl>, lexdec_rt <dbl>, #> #   lexdec_naming_rt <dbl>, prevalence_pknown <dbl>, prevalence_score <dbl>, …

title: lexis Demo
text: Use lex_define() pull Wordset definitions.
code: lex_define(c("bank", "charge"), n = 3) #> # A tibble: 12 × 6 #>    word   speech_part def                                example synonyms labels #>    <chr>  <chr>       <chr>                              <chr>   <chr>    <chr>  #>  1 bank   noun        a flight maneuver                   NA     slip     NA     #>  2 bank   noun        a building in which the business … "the b… bank bu… NA     #>  3 bank   noun        a container, usually with a slot … "I use… money b… NA     #>  4 bank   verb        to have confidence or faith in     "I ban… trust; … NA     #>  5 bank   verb        to cover with ashes so to control… "The f… NA       trans… #>  6 bank   verb        to enclose with a bank             "The h… NA       trans… #>  7 charge noun        a special assignment that is give…  NA     mission  NA     #>  8 charge noun        attention and management implying…  NA     care     NA     #>  9 charge noun        an impetuous rush toward someone … "The b… NA       NA     #> 10 charge verb        to saturate                        "The r… NA       trans… #> 11 charge verb        to energize a battery by passing … "I nee… NA       NA     #> 12 charge verb        to cause formation of a net elect… "charg… NA       NA

title: lexis Demo
text: Get vectors lex_embed(). Nearest neighbors lex_neighbors().
code: emb <- lex_embed(c("dog", "cat", "wolf")) dim(emb) #> [1]  3 50 lex_neighbors("dog", n = 10) #> # A tibble: 10 × 2 #>    word   similarity #>    <chr>       <dbl> #>  1 cat         0.941 #>  2 dogs        0.873 #>  3 boy         0.845 #>  4 pet         0.821 #>  5 rabbit      0.820 #>  6 baby        0.820 #>  7 horse       0.804 #>  8 puppy       0.801 #>  9 mad         0.793 #> 10 monkey      0.787

title: lexis Demo
text: lex_match() finds words matched selected dimensions.
code: lex_match(   targets = c("anger", "joy"),   dims = c("aoa", "concreteness", "valence"),   n = 3 ) #> # A tibble: 6 × 6 #>   target match         distance   aoa concreteness valence #>   <chr>  <chr>            <dbl> <dbl>        <dbl>   <dbl> #> 1 joy    excited          0.221  6.21         2.32    8.11 #> 2 joy    peaceful         0.263  7.22         2.27    8    #> 3 joy    joyful           0.266  7.05         2.12    8.21 #> 4 anger  emergency        0.173  5.94         2.41    2.72 #> 5 anger  frightened       0.193  5.65         2.27    2.45 #> 6 anger  embarrassment    0.203  6.28         2.43    2.72

title: Authors and Citation
text: Jay Timm. Author, maintainer.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Timm J (2026). lexis: English Psycholinguistic Norms Unified Tidy Resource. R package version 0.1.0, https://github.com/jaytimm/lexis.
code: @Manual{,   title = {lexis: English Psycholinguistic Norms in a Unified Tidy Resource},   author = {Jay Timm},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://github.com/jaytimm/lexis}, }

title: lexis
text: lexis R data package collates English psycholinguistic norms multiple published sources common word-level resource, cleaning source files, standardizing word identifiers, harmonizing dimension names scale metadata, aggregating source-specific measures tidy long wide tables. package contains large union vocabulary across norming studies, dictionary entries, GloVe word embeddings, citations construct notes kept alongside data.

title: lexis
text: column lexis_wide (dimension value lexis_long) corresponding row lexis_meta full construct text, scale, original source column, SD availability, citation. notes combine main construct descriptions source datasets.

title: lexis
text: Dimension: aoa Construct: estimated age, years, speaker first understood word someone used front ; linked lexical fluency. Scale: 1–25 years. SD available. Source: Kuperman, V., Stadthagen-Gonzalez, H., & Brysbaert, M. (2012). Age--acquisition ratings 30,000 English words. Behavior Research Methods, 44, 978–990.

title: lexis
text: Dimension: concreteness Construct: degree meaning grounded direct perception action versus abstract, definition-knowledge. Scale: 1–5, abstract concrete. SD available. Source: Brysbaert, M., Warriner, . B., & Kuperman, V. (2014). Concreteness ratings 40 thousand generally known English word lemmas. Frontiers Psychology, 5, 1515.

title: lexis
text: Dimensions: Scale: 1–9 Self-Assessment Manikin. SD available. Source: Warriner, . B., Kuperman, V., & Brysbaert, M. (2013). Norms valence, arousal, dominance 13,915 English lemmas. Behavior Research Methods, 45, 1191–1207.

title: lexis
text: Dimensions: Scale: 0–5 strength perceptual/motor association. SD available. Source: Lynott, D., Connell, L., Brysbaert, M., Brand, J., & Carney, J. (2020). Lancaster sensorimotor norms. Behavior Research Methods, 52, 1271–1291.

title: lexis
text: Dimensions: Scale: reaction time milliseconds. SD available. Source: Balota, D. ., et al. (2007). English Lexicon Project. Behavior Research Methods, 39, 445–459.

title: lexis
text: Dimensions: Scale: prevalence_pknown 0–1; prevalence_score unbounded. SD unavailable. Source: Brysbaert, M., Mandera, P., McCormick, S. F., & Keuleers, E. (2019). Word prevalence norms 62,000 English lemmas. Behavior Research Methods, 51, 1583–1603.

title: lexis
text: Dimension: boi Construct: easily person can physically interact object concept using hands body. Scale: 1–7. SD available. Source: Pexman, P. M., Muraki, E., Sidhu, D. M., Siakaluk, P. D., & Yap, M. J. (2019). Ratings 8,000 English words. Behavior Research Methods, 51, 1134–1146.

title: lexis
text: Dimension: socialness Construct: much meaning concerns people, interaction, roles, institutions, social ideas. Scale: 1–7. SD available. Source: Diveica, V., Pexman, P. M., & Binney, R. J. (2023). Quantifying social semantics. Behavior Research Methods, 55, 461–473.

title: lexis
text: Dimension: ser Construct: strongly reading word evokes sensory experience across modalities. Scale: 1–7. SD unavailable. Source: Juhasz, B. J., & Yap, M. J. (2013). Sensory experience ratings 5,000 mono- disyllabic words. Behavior Research Methods, 45, 111–120.

title: lexis
text: Dimension: humor Construct: funny humor-associated word feels; largely distinct valence, arousal, concreteness. Scale: 1–5. SD available. Source: Engelthaler, T., & Hills, T. T. (2018). Humor norms 4,997 English words. Behavior Research Methods, 50, 1116–1124.

title: lexis
text: Dimension: imageability Construct: ease bringing vivid sensory mental image mind. Scale: transformed 100–700 imageability score, aligned MRC Psycholinguistic Database metric original 1–7 ratings. SD unavailable. Source: Bird, H., Franklin, S., & Howard, D. (2001). Age acquisition imageability ratings large set words, including verbs function words. Behavior Research Methods, Instruments, & Computers, 33(1), 73–79.

title: lexis
text: Dimension: gender_femininity Construct: perceived masculine versus feminine association word’s meaning discourse. Scale: 1–7, masculine feminine. SD available. Source: Roberts, B. E., & Utych, S. M. (2019). Linking gender language: Developing database masculine feminine words. American Politics Research, 47, 1155–1173.

title: lexis
text: Dimensions: Scale: continuous directional association scores. SD unavailable. Source: Meteyard, L., & Vigliocco, G. (2009). Verbs space: Axis direction motion norms 299 English verbs. Behavior Research Methods, 41(2), 565–574.

title: lexis
text: Derived dimensions: Scale: counts. Source: wordset/wordset-dictionary, open English dictionary JSON format. ws_pos included wide format semicolon-separated POS reference list. norming studies present words without sentence context explicit sense disambiguation, Wordset POS entries interpreted identifying rated sense.

title: lexis
text: Dimensions: dense semantic vectors (glove50, glove300) trained large text corpora used distributional semantic similarity. Scale: continuous numeric vector components (50d 300d). Source: Pennington, J., Socher, R., & Manning, C. D. (2014). GloVe: Global vectors word representation. Proceedings 2014 Conference Empirical Methods Natural Language Processing (EMNLP), 1532-1543. Project page: Stanford GloVe.

title: lexis
text: lexis provides lexis_long, lexis_wide, lexis_meta, glove50, wordset_dict.
